name: Umami
what_it_does: Umami is an open-source, self-hostable cookieless web analytics platform with a REST API for querying pageviews, sessions, events, and funnel metrics without using browser cookies or fingerprinting.
use_cases: Retrieve daily pageview and unique visitor counts for a website to power a custom traffic dashboard
Query top referrers and UTM campaign performance for a specific date range to inform content agents
Fetch custom event counts (e.g., button clicks, form submissions) to track conversion funnels programmatically
List all tracked websites and their metadata to enumerate properties in a multi-site content workflow
Pull real-time active session data to trigger alerts or personalization logic based on live traffic spikes
not_for: User-level behavioral analytics or individual session replay — Umami intentionally avoids user tracking
E-commerce attribution with multi-touch models — no built-in revenue or LTV tracking
Large-scale enterprise analytics requiring role-based access controls and audit logs across many teams
best_when: You need a privacy-compliant, cookieless analytics API you can self-host or run on Umami Cloud without vendor lock-in, and your use case is traffic metrics and custom events.
avoid_when: You need individual user journeys, session replay, A/B testing infrastructure, or deep funnel analytics beyond pageviews and custom events.

auth.notes: JWT bearer token obtained via POST /api/auth/login with username and password; tokens expire and must be refreshed. Umami Cloud also supports API keys (v2).

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited on self-hosted; Umami Cloud free tier includes 3 websites, 100k events/month
pricing.paid_tiers: Umami Cloud Pro starts at $9/month for more sites and higher event volumes
pricing.notes: Self-hosting is fully free and open-source (MIT license). Umami Cloud is the managed SaaS option.

performance.rate_limits: No documented rate limits for self-hosted; Umami Cloud limits depend on plan

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors return JSON with a message string but status codes are not always granular; 400 is used broadly for both validation and auth errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: All data retrieval endpoints are read-only and fully idempotent; tracking ingestion is fire-and-forget.
known_agent_gotchas: JWT tokens expire (default 24h) and agents must implement token refresh logic or re-authenticate on 401
Self-hosted instances may have custom base URLs — agents must be configured per deployment, not a fixed endpoint
Date range parameters require Unix timestamps in milliseconds, not seconds or ISO strings
Website IDs are UUIDs assigned at creation; agents must list websites first rather than constructing IDs from names
No webhook support — agents must poll for new data rather than receiving push notifications of events

security_score_components.security_notes: Self-hosted deployments inherit security posture of the host; TLS not guaranteed unless operator configures it. Cookieless design reduces privacy attack surface significantly.
